qualification_assessment.qualification_basis: The term set covers a dense, well-attested inner-being domain. aph (nostril/anger) grounds anger somatically in the breath and face; che.mah (heat/rage) names it as an inner thermal state; cha.rah (to be incensed) names the kindling process; orge (G3709) designates settled dispositional anger as a temperament or inner orientation; thumos (G2372) names the soul/spirit dimension directly in its LSJ entry. Both human and divine anger are extensively attested, with explicit inner location statements.
qualification_assessment.qualification_flag: Several terms in the cluster are predominantly divine-anger registers (cha.ron — always of God's anger per meaning section); several are peripheral (a.phah to bake, it to scream, na.phal to fall); two term blocks are corrupted (thumoō contains lian data; second ka.as entry contains na.ats data). The qualification is not affected by these issues.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: To be angry, enraged; to feel and express strong displeasure and hostility; ranges from petty human anger to righteous anger of God toward sinful disobedience. Active: to provoke to anger, irritate. Passive: to be angry, indignant, enraged.
term_inventory.note: This term block appears under the heading 'anger (parorgismos)' — the heading is a data quality error; the block data clearly belongs to orgizō (G3710). A second instance of this same block also appears under the heading 'to anger (parorgizō)' with different verse citations. The two headings refer to distinct terms but both blocks contain orgizō data.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb hithpael perfective third singular masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To be angry, arrogant. This is a sub-sense (B) of the primary verb a.var (to pass over, cross). The anger meaning is attested in the Hithpael stem. The root cluster covers passing/crossing, ford, arrogance, and fury (ev.rah), revealing that anger is conceptualised as an overflow or crossing-over of inner boundary.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb qal consecutive imperfective third singular masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To be displeased, be angry, fret oneself, be wroth. Qal: to be wroth, be full of wrath, be furious. Hiphil: to provoke to wrath or anger. Hithpael: to put oneself in a rage, anger oneself. Related to qe.tseph (wrath) and the rare qe.tseph meaning splinter/broken twig.

Anger, heat, burning of anger. Meaning section states: always used of God's anger. Related to cha.rah (to be incensed), burning (cho.ri), and ta.cha.rah (to contend). The fire/heat imagery is embedded in the root.
term_inventory.note: Meaning section states 'always used of God's anger' — this limits its applicability to the human inner-being register.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: noun common dual masculine construct / noun common singular masculine construct
term_inventory.meaning_summary: Nostril, nose, face; anger. The term covers the physical organ (nostril/nose), the face as a whole, and anger as the emotional state expressed through the nostrils (flared nostrils, hot breath, smoke from nostrils as a theophanic image). Three STEP sub-entries (H0639G anger, H0639H nose, H0639I face) share identical meaning sections and occurrence counts — these are different contextual uses of the same term.
term_inventory.note: The term appears under three separate STEP headings (face H0639I, face: anger H0639G, face: nose H0639H) all with the same occurrence count (278) and related words. These are not distinct terms but three anchor-token sub-senses of a single entry. Consolidated here as one term.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Outpouring, overflow, excess, fury, wrath, arrogance. Sub-definitions: overflow/excess/outburst; arrogance; overflowing rage or fury. Related to a.var (to pass, be angry), ford terms, and side/beyond terms — anger as an overflow crossing its boundary.
Anger, indignation. Related to verbal form za.am (be indignant). Appears in verse set primarily in divine-anger contexts but also in the prophet's own filled-indignation (Jer 15:17).

Heat, rage, hot displeasure, indignation, anger, wrath, poison, bottles. Sub-definitions: heat (fever; venom/poison figuratively); burning anger/rage. Aramaic equivalent: che.ma (H2528). The semantic overlap of heat, venom, and rage is a significant inner-being observation.
Anger, rage. Aramaic form of che.mah (H2534). Shares the same related words cluster.
term_inventory.note: Occurrence count mirrors che.mah (H2534) — these may be counted together in the source. Only 2 verses provided, both from Daniel (Aramaic sections).

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Wind, breath, mind, spirit — specifically the sub-sense of temper/anger (1c3) as one of many inner dispositions. The full meaning range of ruach covers breath, wind, spirit as life-principle, spirit as seat of emotion, seat of mental acts, seat of moral character, and the Spirit of God. The anger sub-sense (J) is a small but significant subset: spirit as 'that which breathes quickly in animation or agitation' including temper, anger, impatience, and patience.
term_inventory.note: The anchor here is specifically sub-sense J (temper). This is a single sub-sense of the 181-entry ruach — not a standalone anger term but a contextual use. The verse set (Judg 8:3, Pro 14:29, 17:27, 25:28, 29:11, Ecc 7:9, 10:4, Mic 2:7) specifically addresses temper management and cool/hot spirit as inner-being states.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Wrath, anger. Sub-definitions: of God (1a); of man (1b). Related to qa.tsaph (be angry) and qe.tseph the splinter (H7110B).

Splinter, twig, broken twig. Meaning stated as uncertain. Single occurrence in Hos 10:7. Related to qa.tsaph (be angry) and qe.tseph wrath (H7110A) — the connection between wrath and a broken twig may be etymological (the snapping of something) but this is not stated in the source.
term_inventory.note: Peripheral to the inner-being anger analysis; recorded for completeness.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: To bake; baker; to be baked. Included in the STEP anger cluster via root adjacency to aph (nostril/anger). The shared root connection is the shared consonant cluster — a.phah (bake) and aph (nose/anger) are treated as root relatives in STEP.
term_inventory.note: Peripheral to the inner-being anger analysis. The sole potentially relevant verse is Hos 7:6 ('all night their anger smolders; in the morning it blazes like a flaming fire') which uses baking/oven imagery for anger but does not contain a.phah as its primary anger term.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb qal imperfective third singular masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To be hot, furious, burn, become angry, be kindled. Qal: to burn, kindle anger. Niphal: to be angry with, be incensed. Hiphil: to burn, kindle. Hithpael: to heat oneself in vexation. Related to cha.ron (burning anger), burning (cho.ri), cha.rar (to scorch), and ta.cha.rah (to contend).

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb tiphil imperfective second singular masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To burn, be kindled (of anger); Tiphel: to heat oneself in vexation. Only 2 occurrences. Related to cha.rah (to be incensed), cha.ron (burning anger), burning (cho.ri).
term_inventory.note: Verses provided (Jer 12:5, 22:15) use the word in the sense of competing or racing — the anger/vexation sense is not explicit in these English translations.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb haphel perfective third plural masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: Aphel: to rage, enrage. Aramaic form of ra.gaz (H7264, to tremble). The anger/rage sense and the trembling sense share the same root — the connection between inner agitation/trembling and anger is embedded in the etymology.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb hiphil imperfective first singular either gender
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To fall, lie, be cast down, fail (sub-sense K: angry/fallen face). The anger connection is through the idiom of the face falling — the same verb used for falling prostrate or falling in battle is used for the countenance/face falling in anger or dejection. Only 1 verse provided (Jer 3:12).
term_inventory.note: Only 1 verse provided; the anger sense is specifically the face-falling idiom. The 446 occurrences belong overwhelmingly to the primary sense (to fall). Thin data for the anger sub-sense.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb qal imperfective second singular masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To keep, keep guard, reserve, maintain. The anger connection is through the idiom of keeping/guarding anger — holding a grudge or retaining wrath. Aramaic equivalent: ne.tar (H5202). Verse set: Lev 19:18 (do not bear a grudge), Psa 103:9 (he will not keep his anger forever), Jer 3:5 (will he be indignant to the end?), and Song 8:11-12 (vineyard keepers).
term_inventory.note: The primary meaning is to keep/guard; the anger sense is a secondary idiom (to keep/guard anger = to bear a grudge). Relevant inner-being verses are Lev 19:18 and Psa 103:9.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: To sharpen; metaphorically to incite, stir up; to irritate, provoke, anger. Intransitive: to be greatly distressed or angered. LSJ: to urge, spur on, stimulate; to provoke, irritate; passive: to be provoked; of sicknesses, to grow virulent. Related to oxus (sharp/swift), para (from/with/beside), and paroxusmos (stirring up).

To be angry, be vexed, be indignant, be wroth, be grieved, provoke to anger and wrath. Qal: to be vexed/indignant; to be angry. Piel: to provoke to anger. Hiphil: to vex; to vex/provoke to anger. Related to ka.as (vexation noun, H3708) and ka.a.s (vexation noun, H3708B).

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb qal consecutive imperfective third singular masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To scream, shriek. Related to to pounce (it H5860 alt sense), bird of prey (a.yit). Single occurrence in 1Sa 25:14 where Nabal railed at David's messengers.
term_inventory.note: Peripheral to the inner-being anger analysis. The screaming/shrieking sense is an expression of anger but the term does not name an inner state.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb piel perfective third plural either gender
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To spurn, contemn, despise, abhor. Qal: to spurn, contemn. Piel: to spurn; to cause to contemn. Hiphil: to spurn. Hithpolel: to be contemned. Related to contempt (ne.a.tsah) and to flee (na.tsah).
term_inventory.note: Included in the anger cluster as a related inner-being state (contemptuous spurning as an expression or cause of anger); the term primarily names a disposition of contempt rather than anger itself.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: To be hot, become warm. Qal: to be or grow warm; of passion (figuratively). Niphal: to become aroused, inflame oneself with. Piel: to warm. Hithpael: to warm oneself. Included via the heat/passion connection to the anger cluster — the Qal and Niphal can name passionate inner arousal.
term_inventory.note: Most verses provided (1Ki 1:1-2, Job 39:14, Ecc 4:11, Isa 44:16) are physical warmth contexts. The anger-relevant verses are Deu 19:6 (hot anger of blood avenger) and Psa 39:3 (heart became hot within me). The passion sub-sense is significant but not the dominant usage.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Agitation, excitement, raging, trouble, turmoil, trembling. Sub-definitions: turmoil/disquiet/raging; trembling/trepidation. Related to ra.gaz (to tremble), quivering (rag.gaz, rog.zah).
Anger, vexation, provocation, grief. Sub-definitions: vexation of men; vexation of God; vexation/grief/frustration. Related to ka.as (verb, to provoke) and rock (keph).

term_inventory.note: DATA QUALITY: The second vexation (ka.as) term block is corrupted — it contains the word analysis data for na.ats (H5006, to spurn) rather than ka.as noun data. The verses listed under this heading do belong to the ka.as / vexation semantic field. No grammatical form, occurrence count, or meaning range for ka.as (H3708A) can be recovered from this block.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Wrath, anger; the feeling and expression of strong displeasure and hostility; ranges from petty human anger to righteous anger of God. Primarily mental bent, impulse; anger, indignation, wrath; vengeance, punishment. LSJ: natural impulse or propensity, hence temperament, disposition, mood — the primary sense is of a settled inner disposition before it narrows to anger specifically.

Wrath, fury, anger, rage; a state of intense displeasure based in real or perceived wrong. Primarily the soul, mind; hence a strong passion or emotion of the mind. LSJ: soul, spirit as the principle of life, feeling, and thought — covering breath/life, spirit/strength, desire/appetite, mind/temper/will, spirit/courage, seat of anger, heart as seat of emotions (joy, grief, fear, love), and seat of thought.

term_inventory.note: DATA QUALITY: The thumoō term block is corrupted — it contains the word analysis data for the adverb lian (G3029, 'greatly', 13 occurrences) rather than thumoō data. The single verse listed (Mat 2:16, Herod's furious rage) does belong to thumoō. No grammatical form, occurrence count, or meaning range for thumoō can be recovered from this block.

DATA QUALITY: mēnis (vehement anger) appears in the STEP related word list but has no term block in the source file. No Strong's number, grammatical form, occurrence count, meaning range, or verses are provided.

DATA QUALITY: enkotēma (an object of anger) appears in the STEP related word list but has no term block in the source file. No Strong's number, grammatical form, occurrence count, meaning range, or verses are provided.

DATA QUALITY: cholos (anger) appears in the STEP related word list but has no term block in the source file. No Strong's number, grammatical form, occurrence count, meaning range, or verses are provided.

DATA QUALITY: parorgismos appears as a heading in the source file but the word analysis block under it contains orgizō data (G3710), not parorgismos data. parorgismos is also listed as a related word under orgizō/orgē. It appears in Eph 4:26 ('do not let the sun go down on your anger') — the term in that verse is parorgismos. No independent term data can be recovered from the source file.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Data from this block is identical to orgizō (G3710) — the block is corrupted. parorgizō is a prefixed form (para + orgizō) meaning to provoke to anger or to anger alongside/further. Verses listed: Rom 10:19 (make you angry), Eph 6:4 (do not provoke your children to anger).
term_inventory.note: DATA QUALITY: The term block for parorgizō contains the orgizō word analysis (G3710) data — same corruption as the parorgismos heading. The occurrence count of 8 and meaning section belong to orgizō, not parorgizō. The two verses listed (Rom 10:19, Eph 6:4) do appear to belong to parorgizō.

orgizō.reason: Divine/royal judgment register — king's anger as an executive act, not purely an inner-being state.
Eschatological-divine register; nations raging and God's wrath as cosmic event.

a.var.reason: Divine response to national sin — corporate/divine register.
Divine anger expressed in historical judgment — extended register.
Verse is about meddling and grabbing a dog by the ears — the anger connection is not explicit in the English text; may be anchored to an adjacent word in the Hebrew.

qa.tsaph.reason: Corporate/ritual register — wrath coming upon the congregation.
National historical summary — provoking God to wrath over a generation.
Warning about corporate anger of God.

cha.ron.reason: Verse about thorns and pots heating — the anger connection is not explicit in the English text.

aph_nose.reason: Creation narrative — breath of life through nostrils; the somatic-anger dimension is background here.
Breath of life in nostrils of creatures — no anger referent.
Blast of God's nostrils as theophanic power — the somatic image is anger-adjacent.
Smoke from God's nostrils as theophanic anger — somatic anger image.
Blast of God's nostrils — theophanic power register.
Breath in human nostrils as marker of frailty — no anger referent.

aph_face.reason: Sweat of the face — labour context, no anger referent.

ev.rah.reason: Divine-historical register — plague as expression of anger.
Day of wrath as eschatological-judicial category.
Wrath as outcome for the wicked — judicial register.
Verse names the scoffer/arrogant man — the ev.rah link may be via the arrogance sub-sense of the term rather than fury directly.
Day of the Lord — eschatological-divine register.

za.am.reason: Divine instruments of judgment — extended register.
Divine fury passing by — extended register.
Verse about treacherous bow and princes falling by the sword — the za.am connection is not explicit in the English text.

che.mah.reason: Venom of beasts — literal poison sense of che.mah, not anger.
Poison of serpents — literal venom sense.
Venom like a serpent — literal poison sense.

ru.ach_temper.reason: God stirring up the anger of foreign nations — divine causative register.
Question about whether the Lord has grown impatient — the impatience/temper sense of ruach is present but framed as a rhetorical question about God's character.

qe.tseph_wrath.reason: Corporate-cultic register — protecting the congregation from wrath.
Corporate-judicial register — wrath going out from God.

cha.rah.reason: Eschatological-promise register — those incensed will be shamed.

na.tar.reason: Vineyard keeper — literal keeping sense, no anger referent.
Vineyard keeper — literal keeping sense, no anger referent.

na.ats.reason: Consequence statement — those who despised will not see the land.

cha.mam.reason: Physical warmth — elderly David unable to get warm.
Physical warmth — Elisha warming the child's flesh.
Ostrich eggs warming on the ground — no anger referent.
Two people keeping warm — no anger referent.

ro.gez.reason: Prayer for divine action — wrath is referenced but the register is intercessory rather than descriptive of an inner state.

ka.as_vexation.reason: Divine response to national provocation — extended register.
Sorrow/sadness making the heart glad — the vexation/anger sense is secondary to a wisdom observation about sorrow.

orgē.reason: Divine judicial register — wrath remaining on the unbeliever.
Cosmic-divine register — wrath revealed from heaven.

thumos.reason: Eschatological register — wine of passion of Babylon's immorality.

qe.tseph_splinter.reason: Image of a king perishing like a twig on water — the splinter/broken twig sense rather than the anger sense.

ta.cha.rah.reason: Racing with horses — the anger/vexation sense is not explicit in the English text.
Competing in cedar — the anger/vexation sense is not explicit in the English text.

na.phal_angry.reason: Divine promise not to look in anger — the face-falling idiom is the anger connection, but the verse is a divine-mercy statement.

a.phah.reason: Oven imagery for smouldering/blazing anger — the anger is named through the oven metaphor; a.phah itself names the baking action, not the anger.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: Multiple terms — cha.ron, ev.rah, za.am, qe.tseph, orgē in divine register
cross_registry_links.note: The anger cluster carries an extremely large divine-anger register; if divine wrath is treated as a separate category in Framework B there will be significant term overlap with the human-anger inner-being analysis.

ruach appears in this cluster specifically as the seat of temper/anger; it is a 181 entry in its own right. The anger sub-sense of ruach (cool spirit / hasty temper) should be cross-referenced in both entries.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: makrothumōs (patiently) — related word under thumos; 'slow to anger' (aph) idiom
cross_registry_links.note: The slow-to-anger (arek aph) and long-suffering (makrothumia) concepts are the positive counterparts to the anger cluster; thumos generates the makro- compound family. These may be separate entries in the 181.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: ka.as / ka.a.s — anger shading into grief and vexation
cross_registry_links.note: ka.as and ka.a.s span anger, vexation, and grief — the vexation entries may overlap with grief or distress entries in the 181.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: che.mah appears alongside jealousy in Num 25:11; Eze 16:38, 16:42
cross_registry_links.note: che.mah (heat/rage) frequently appears in compound with jealousy vocabulary — anger and jealousy share the heat metaphor in Hebrew. If jealousy is a separate 181 entry the two will share verses and root families.

The anger sense of aph is inseparable from the face/countenance sense — anger is read on the face and expressed through the nostrils. If face/countenance is a separate 181 entry there will be direct term overlap.
na.ats is included in the anger cluster as a related inner-being state; if contempt or spurning is a separate 181 entry the cross-reference should be noted.

data_quality_flags.description: The heading 'anger (parorgismos)' contains the word analysis block for orgizō (G3710), not parorgismos. parorgismos (G3686) is the term in Eph 4:26 ('do not let the sun go down on your anger'). The block's grammatical form (Verb Present Middle Imperative 2nd Plural) and occurrence count (8) belong to orgizō. parorgismos data cannot be recovered from this source file.

The heading 'to anger (parorgizō)' also contains orgizō word analysis data (Verb Aorist Passive Indicative 3rd Singular, 8 occurrences). The verses (Rom 10:19, Eph 6:4) appear to belong to parorgizō. The block is a corruption of the same type as the parorgismos entry above.

The heading 'to anger (thumoō)' contains the word analysis block for the adverb lian (G3029, 'greatly', 13 occurrences). thumoō (G2363) data — grammatical form, occurrence count, meaning range — cannot be recovered from this block. The single verse (Mat 2:16) belongs to thumoō.

The second 'vexation (ka.as)' heading contains the word analysis block for na.ats (H5006, to spurn, 25 occurrences). ka.as noun (H3708A) data cannot be recovered from this block. The verses listed under this heading do belong to the ka.as / vexation semantic field.
mēnis (vehement anger) appears in the STEP related word list but has no term block, Strong's number, grammatical form, occurrence count, or verses in the source file.
enkotēma (an object of anger) appears in the STEP related word list but has no term block in the source file.
cholos (anger) appears in the STEP related word list but has no term block in the source file.

na.phal (H5307K, to fall: angry) has 446 total occurrences but only 1 verse provided (Jer 3:12). The anger sub-sense is the face-falling idiom — a specific and significant idiom (Gen 4:5-6 is the classic instance) but not represented by its own verse set here.
re.gaz (H7265, to enrage) has 43 occurrences but only 1 verse provided (Ezr 5:12).

aph appears under three separate STEP headings (face H0639I, face:anger H0639G, face:nose H0639H) all with the same occurrence count (278) and identical related words. These are STEP sub-entry anchor senses of the same term, not three distinct terms. Consolidated to one term entry with verse sets distinguished by sub-sense.
a.var (H5674B, be angry) has an anchor-token artifact 'his (hu - hu)' preceding the term heading; does not corrupt term data.
ev.rah has an anchor-token artifact 'and (v - /v)' preceding the term heading; does not corrupt term data.
che.mah has anchor-token artifacts 'you (kem), with (im)' preceding the term heading; does not corrupt term data.
